items.q: Why is only about 10% of energy passed to the next trophic level?
items.correct: Most energy is lost as heat through respiration and in undigested material, so little is available to the next level.
items.misconception: All the energy is passed on with nothing lost.
items.random_wrong: Energy is created at each trophic level.
items.why: Students assume energy transfers fully, ignoring large losses through respiration and waste.

items.q: Are invasive species always more harmful than native species?
items.correct: Not inherently, but they can be very damaging where they lack natural predators and outcompete native species.
items.misconception: Native species are always the harmful ones.
items.random_wrong: Invasive species are always beneficial to ecosystems.
items.why: Learners over-generalise rather than recognising harm depends on context and lack of natural controls.

items.q: Does high biodiversity make an ecosystem more stable?
items.correct: Generally yes; greater diversity often gives more resilience to disturbance, though it is not guaranteed.
items.misconception: No; biodiversity has no link to ecosystem stability.
items.random_wrong: Biodiversity only counts the number of plants.
items.why: Students dismiss the diversity-stability link or define biodiversity too narrowly.

items.q: The variety of living organisms in an area, from genes to ecosystems, is called:
items.why: Correct answer: Biodiversity. “Carrying capacity” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Green plants that make their own food sit at which trophic level?
items.why: Correct answer: Producers. “Primary consumers” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A species whose loss would dramatically alter its whole ecosystem is a:
items.why: It's Keystone species. “Pioneer species” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A network of interconnected food chains is best described as a:
items.why: It's Food web. “A single food chain” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
